section.title: I Give You Thanks with My Whole Heart
section.sectionRationale: The first of the eight closing Davidic psalms that gather Book V toward its finale, this individual thanksgiving pairs personal gratitude for answered prayer with a vision of kings of the earth one day praising the LORD, David's confidence resting not on his own strength but on God's exalted name and word.
section.thesis: David's wholehearted thanksgiving for a prayer already answered opens into confidence that the LORD who exalts His name and word above all things will finish the work He has begun, a pattern of praise, humility, and settled trust fulfilled in Christ, before whom every king will one day bow.
section.frameNote: Three movements: wholehearted thanksgiving before every power for an answered prayer, verses 1-3; the LORD's exalted name reaching the kings of the earth even as He stoops to the lowly, verses 4-6; and confidence that He will finish what He has begun, verses 7-8.

units.label: Wholehearted thanks for an answered prayer

verses.text: I give You thanks with all my heart; before the gods I sing Your praises.
I bow down toward Your holy temple and give thanks to Your name for Your {{chesed|loving devotion}} and Your faithfulness; You have exalted Your name and Your word above all else.
On the day I called, You answered me; You emboldened me and strengthened my soul.

units.label: The LORD's name reaches the kings of the earth
units.note: high, yet near to the lowly

verses.text: All the kings of the earth will give You thanks, O LORD, when they hear the words of Your mouth.
They will sing of the ways of the LORD, for the glory of the LORD is great.
Though the LORD is on high, He attends to the {{shaphal|lowly}}; but the proud He knows from afar.

units.label: Confidence He will finish His purpose

verses.text: If I walk in the midst of trouble, You preserve me from the anger of my foes; You extend Your hand, and Your right hand saves me.
The LORD will fulfill His purpose for me. O LORD, Your loving devotion endures forever, do not abandon the works of Your hands.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion, God's loyal love that does not quit. David thanks God for chesed paired with faithfulness, the two words that most often summarize the LORD's covenant character, Exodus 34:6.

shaphal.gloss: Low, humble, brought down. God's high exaltation does not distance Him from the lowly; instead He draws near to them, while keeping the proud at a distance, a pattern echoed throughout Scripture, Isaiah 57:15, James 4:6.

basic.context: David gives thanks for a specific answered prayer, likely a victory that drew the attention of surrounding kings, and grounds his gratitude not in the outcome itself but in the character of the God who answered, whose name and word He has exalted above everything else.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, specific thanksgiving for an actual answered prayer, yet its logic generalizes: because God has proven faithful once, He can be trusted to finish what He has started. The psalm describes David's own experience; it prescribes a pattern of grounding present confidence in past answered prayer.
basic.originalLanguages: David thanks God for His chesed, steadfast covenant love, paired with faithfulness, the classic pairing that summarizes the LORD's character since Exodus 34:6. Though high above all, God attends to the shaphal, the lowly, while keeping His distance from the proud, a reversal of worldly power that runs through both Testaments.
basic.typology: The vision of all the kings of the earth giving God thanks, verse 4, anticipates the day every knee bows to Jesus, Philippians 2:10-11, and the nations streaming to worship in the new creation, Revelation 21:24.
basic.christ: David's confidence that the LORD will fulfill His purpose for him, verse 8, is fulfilled supremely in Christ, who declared on the cross, It is finished, John 19:30, and who, though exalted above all, drew near to the lowly throughout His earthly ministry, Matthew 11:29, Philippians 2:6-8.
basic.god: God is shown as exalted above every rival power, yet personally attentive, answering prayer the very day it is offered, drawing near to the humble, and faithfully finishing the work He begins in those who are His.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 1:6 echoes verse 8's confidence that God will complete His good work. Philippians 2:9-11 fulfills the vision of every king giving God thanks. James 4:6 restates the LORD's nearness to the humble and distance from the proud. John 19:30 is the ultimate fulfillment of the LORD finishing His purpose.
basic.oneStory: David's thanksgiving for an answered prayer becomes confidence that the God who is high above all, yet near to the lowly, will finish what He has begun, a pattern completed in Christ, before whom every king will one day give thanks and in whom God's saving purpose is finished forever.

context.literary: A personal thanksgiving psalm in three stanzas, moving from private gratitude, verses 1-3, to a public vision of the kings of the earth praising God, verses 4-6, to renewed confidence amid ongoing trouble, verses 7-8.
context.historical: Attributed to David, likely composed after a specific deliverance or victory that brought recognition from surrounding rulers, in the pattern of 2 Samuel 8, where David's victories caused neighboring kings to acknowledge the LORD.
context.canonical: The first of the closing Davidic collection, Psalms 138-145, that gathers Book V and the whole Psalter toward its final crescendo of praise in Psalms 146-150.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give thanks publicly for a specific answered prayer, to ground that thanksgiving in God's exalted yet near character, and to draw from it settled confidence that God will complete His purposes for His servant.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own experience of answered prayer and ongoing trouble; it prescribes the abiding pattern of wholehearted, public thanksgiving that becomes the ground for confident trust in future trials.

debates.question: Who are the gods before whom David sings praise in verse 1?

views.label: Heavenly beings or angels, understood as a court before whom the LORD's supremacy is declared
views.case: Other psalms use similar language for a heavenly assembly, Psalm 82:1, 89:6-7, so David sings praise even in the presence of exalted spiritual beings, showing the LORD's supremacy over them.

views.label: The false gods or idols of the nations, sung of with deliberate irony
views.case: The Old Testament often names other nations' gods this way to underscore their powerlessness before the LORD, so David declares his praise even in view of every rival claim to deity.

debates.bearing: Either reading exalts the LORD above every other power, spiritual or idolatrous, reinforcing verse 2's claim that God has exalted His name and word above all else.

study.originalLanguages: David thanks God for chesed, steadfast covenant love, paired with faithfulness, echoing Exodus 34:6. The LORD, though exalted, attends to the shaphal, the lowly, keeping the proud at a distance, the same reversal Scripture consistently attributes to God's character, Isaiah 57:15, James 4:6.

typology.type: All the kings of the earth giving God thanks, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: Every knee bowing to Jesus, Philippians 2:10-11, and the nations bringing their glory into the new Jerusalem, Revelation 21:24.

typology.type: The LORD fulfilling His purpose for David, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Christ's finished work of redemption, John 19:30, and God's ongoing faithfulness to complete the good work He begins in believers, Philippians 1:6.

study.christ: David's confidence that the LORD will fulfill His purpose for him, verse 8, is fulfilled in Christ's declaration, It is finished, John 19:30, and in His pattern of exalted yet humble nearness to the lowly, Matthew 11:29, Philippians 2:6-8, the same reversal this psalm celebrates in God's own character.
study.god: God is exalted above every rival power yet personally attentive, answering prayer swiftly, drawing near to the humble rather than the proud, and faithfully completing His purposes for those who trust Him.

crossReferences.note: Echoes verse 8's confidence that God who has begun a good work will bring it to completion.
Fulfills the psalm's vision that every king, indeed every knee, will one day give thanks to God through Christ.
Restates the psalm's pattern that God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
Christ's own words, It is finished, are the ultimate fulfillment of the LORD finishing His purpose for His people.

study.oneStory: David's thanksgiving for one answered prayer becomes confidence that the God who is high above all yet near to the lowly will finish what He has begun, a pattern completed in Christ, before whom every king will one day give thanks and in whom God's saving purpose stands finished forever.

sermon.title: Above All Things
sermon.why: Begg expounds Psalm 138:2's declaration that God has exalted His name and word above all things, explaining how God's self-revelation through creation, conscience, His Word, and His Son strips away pride and leads to praise, directly serving this psalm's opening confession.

quiz.q: What specifically prompts David's thanksgiving in verses 1-3?

options.text: A prayer he called out that the LORD answered, emboldening and strengthening his soul on that very day.
options.note: Verse 3 ties the thanksgiving directly to a specific answered prayer.

options.text: A general feeling of gratitude with no particular cause.
options.note: The text specifies a concrete answered prayer, not a vague mood of thankfulness.

options.text: The building of a new temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: David bows toward the temple in worship, but the specific cause of thanksgiving is an answered prayer, not a construction project.

options.text: A treaty signed with a foreign king.
options.note: The psalm implies recognition by foreign kings will follow, verse 4, but the immediate cause of thanks is God's answer to prayer.

quiz.q: What has God exalted above all else, according to verse 2?

options.text: His name and His word.
options.note: Verse 2 states plainly that God has exalted His name and His word above all else.

options.text: The wealth and armies of Israel.
options.note: The text exalts God's name and word, not Israel's military or material strength.

options.text: The temple building itself above the LORD's own character.
options.note: David bows toward the temple, but what is exalted above all is God's name and word, not the structure.

options.text: David's own kingship over other nations.
options.note: The psalm's focus is God's self-revelation and character, not David's political standing.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe God's relationship to the lowly and the proud in verse 6?

options.text: Though the LORD is on high, He attends to the lowly, but the proud He knows from afar.
options.note: This exact reversal, God's nearness to the humble and distance from the proud, is the verse's central claim.

options.text: God treats the lowly and the proud in exactly the same way.
options.note: The verse draws a clear distinction; God draws near the lowly and distances the proud.

options.text: God ignores both the lowly and the proud equally.
options.note: The verse says God attends to the lowly, the opposite of ignoring them.

options.text: God rewards the proud for their confidence and achievement.
options.note: The verse says the opposite, that God knows the proud only from a distance.

quiz.q: What confidence does David express in verse 8 about God's purpose for him?

options.text: That the LORD will fulfill His purpose for him and asks God not to abandon the work of His own hands.
options.note: Verse 8 states this confidence directly, grounding it in God's enduring loving devotion.

options.text: That David must complete his own purpose entirely through his own effort.
options.note: The verse credits the LORD, not David's own effort, with fulfilling His purpose.

options.text: That David's troubles are now permanently over.
options.note: Verse 7 assumes ongoing trouble; the confidence is about God's preserving and completing work within it, not the absence of trouble.

options.text: That future generations will have to finish what David could not.
options.note: The verse expresses confidence in God's own faithfulness to finish the work, not a deferral to later generations.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's vision of kings giving God thanks connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates every knee bowing to Jesus and every tongue confessing Him as Lord.
options.note: Philippians 2:10-11 fulfills the psalm's picture of universal acknowledgment of God's supremacy.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: The psalm's vision of universal praise is directly picked up in passages like Philippians 2 and Revelation 21.

options.text: It predicts a specific political alliance in the first century.
options.note: The psalm speaks of ultimate worship and acknowledgment of God, not a particular historical treaty.

options.text: It is fulfilled only by the physical conquest of foreign kings by Israel's armies.
options.note: The fulfillment is worship and acknowledgment of God's greatness, not military conquest, culminating in Christ's universal lordship.

questions: David thanks God wholeheartedly for one specific answered prayer. What has God done for you recently that deserves the same wholehearted thanks?
David exalts God's name and word above everything else. What competes for that place of highest exaltation in your life right now?
God draws near to the lowly and keeps the proud at a distance. Where is pride keeping you at a distance from God's nearness this week?
David trusts that the LORD will finish the purpose He has begun, even amid ongoing trouble. What unfinished situation in your life needs that same confidence today?
This psalm anticipates kings of the earth one day giving God thanks, fulfilled in Christ before whom every knee will bow. How does that future certainty shape how you live now?

translationNotes.note: Before the gods can refer either to heavenly beings in God's court, as in Psalm 82:1, or ironically to the false gods of the nations; either reading exalts the LORD above every rival power.
You emboldened me and strengthened my soul carries a footnote possibility, You emboldened me with strength in my soul, both readings pointing to inward courage granted the day David called.

The BSB text joins Your loving devotion endures forever to do not abandon the works of Your hands with an em dash in some printings; here the two clauses are read as a single flowing petition grounded in God's enduring covenant love.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com (local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error). The BSB's em dash in verse 8 was converted to a comma per house style. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's 'Above All Things' at truthforlife.org, Scripture Psalm 138:2.
